g.data-narration: Welcome to Branch Instructions in 8051. These instructions enable decision-making and flow control in microcontroller programs.
Branch instructions modify the Program Counter to alter sequential execution flow, enabling loops and conditional logic.
g.text: Instructions that alter sequential execution by modifying
the Program Counter (PC) to transfer control.

g.data-narration: Branch instructions create responsive embedded systems that can make decisions and handle complex algorithmic operations efficiently.
g.text: Essential for implementing loops, conditionals, subroutines, and

g.data-narration: Branch instructions are classified into unconditional jumps, conditional jumps, and subroutine calls for different control needs.
AJMP transfers within 2KB, LJMP accesses entire 64KB, and SJMP uses relative offset from -128 to +127 bytes.
JZ jumps when accumulator equals zero; JNZ jumps when accumulator contains any non-zero value.
JC tests carry flag set (CY=1) while JNC branches when carry is cleared (CY=0).
JB tests a specific bit, JNB branches if bit is clear, while JBC also clears the bit after branching.
CJNE compares two operands and branches if not equal, setting carry flag based on unsigned comparison result.
g.text: CJNE - Compare and Jump if Not Equal
Three variants compare values and branch if not equal:
After CJNE: C=0 if source > dest | C=1 if source < dest (unsigned)

g.data-narration: DJNZ decrements first then checks for zero, making it ideal for loop control in 8051 assembly programs.
g.text: DJNZ - Decrement and Jump if Not Zero
Operation: Decrement operand → Check result → Branch if not zero

g.data-narration: ACALL and LCALL push return address onto stack; RET and RETI restore execution to the calling point.
PSW register flags - Carry, Auxiliary Carry, Overflow, and Parity - control conditional branch behavior.
This example uses DJNZ to create a loop that sums numbers from 1 to 10, producing result 55 in accumulator.
g.text: LOOP: ADD A, R0 ; Add current number
DJNZ R0, LOOP ; Decrement, jump if ≠0
• Loop: Add R0 to A, decrement R0

g.data-narration: This example uses CJNE to compare two memory values and stores appropriate results based on equality comparison.
g.rect:
NOT_EQUAL:
Logic Flow: 1. Load A from memory 30H 2. CJNE: branch if 30H ≠ 31H 3. If equal: store 0FFH, skip NOT_EQUAL

g.data-narration: This example demonstrates creating and calling a subroutine to convert binary to BCD format using LCALL and RET.
g.text: Example 3: Subroutine for Binary to BCD
• SWAP A: move tens to upper nibble

g.data-narration: Remember: AJMP/ACALL reach 2KB, LJMP/LCALL reach full 64KB, SJMP uses relative ±127 bytes range.
Avoid common errors: initialize SP before CALL, use RETI in interrupts, remember DJNZ decrements first.
Key exam points: CJNE sets carry flag, DJNZ is loop control, RETI restores interrupt enable flip-flop after ISR.
g.text: • CJNE affects C: source < dest → C=1 | source > dest → C=0
• DJNZ: decrement first, then check for zero - key for loop control
• All conditional jumps (except JB/JNB/JBC) use relative addressing (-128 to +127)

g.data-narration: Master branch instructions to create efficient 8051 programs with proper flow control, loops, and subroutines for embedded applications.
g.text: Master Branch Instructions for Embedded Success!
Practice with examples and understand flag dependencies for exam success.
